rules.rule_statement: The majority of states require equitable division when dividing a couple’s assets upon divorce. Under the equitable division system of property division, marital property is divided fairly, but not necessarily equally. Community property is an alternative system of dividing marital property adopted in a minority of states, including California. These states essentially treat a marriage as a partnership, or a “community,” that acquires assets and debts on behalf of the spouses. Rule In community property states, assets and debts acquired in that state by either spouse during the marriage are presumed to be community property. Collectively, community property assets and debts form the community estate (i.e., “marital community estate,” “community property estate,” “marital community,” or “marital economic community”). California generally requires an equal division of community property at divorce unless an enforceable property settlement agreement provides otherwise. It is important to be familiar with the basic terminology underlying the concepts of this property system: Presumed to include all property and earnings acquired (and debts incurred) by a spouse during marriage while domiciled in California Community Property (CP) Non-community property belonging to one spouse; includes: Separate Property property acquired by a spouse before the marriage or after permanent separation or divorce and all property acquired by a spouse via gift, bequest, devise, or descent, even if acquired during the marriage (SP) Property acquired by a spouse while living in a noncommunity-property state that would be characterized as CP if the couple had been living in California when the property was acquired Quasi-Community Property (QCP) Quasi-Marital Property acquired by putative spouses during their putative marriage (See II.C. Putative Spouse Doctrine, infra.) Property (QMP) Changing the classification of property by agreement or transfer from CP to SP, SP to CP or one spouse’s SP to the other spouse’s SP Transmutation Process of determining the source of funds used to acquire an asset to establish its classification as CP or SP Tracing Community property essays often involve (1) a timeline of events and transactions related to a marriage and (2) a call of the question asking you to discuss the parties’ rights and liabilities regarding a list of items, assets, or debts. rules.rule_statement: Quasi-community-property classification is triggered by divorce or the death of the titled spouse. When a couple changes their domicile to California, the property rights that they have from the prior domicile remain intact during their marriage. Therefore, the property does not transform into community property just because the married parties moved to California. However, if the parties divorce or the spouse who holds title to the property dies, then California law applies, and the property is treated like community property.

rules.source_text:
Quasi-Community Property
Quasi-community-property classification is triggered by divorce or the death of the titled spouse. When a couple changes their domicile to California, the property rights that they have from the prior domicile remain intact during their marriage. Therefore, the property does not transform into community property just because the married parties moved to California. However, if the parties divorce or the spouse who holds title to the property dies, then California law applies, and the property is treated like community property.

rules.rule_statement: A change in the form of an asset does not change that asset’s characterization. Therefore, tracing an asset acquired during a marriage back to a separate property source (e.g., separate property funds) can be used to rebut the presumption that the asset is community property. Example 1: During marriage, Husband purchased a boat for $25,000. The boat, which was untitled, is presumed community property because it was acquired during marriage. The source of funds used to buy the boat was $25,000 cash inherited from his late uncle’s estate. Inherited property is characterized as separate property of the recipient spouse. A change in form of the asset from cash to a tangible asset will not change its characterization. Therefore, the boat is the separate property of Husband. Tracing the source of funds used to acquire the boat to Husband’s separate property will rebut the general community-property presumption. When property is acquired with both separate and community property, the acquired property is typically part separate property and part community property based on the value of each of type of property used to acquire that property (i.e., pro-rata or pro tanto allocation) (see VII.C.4. Apportioning Interest of Community Property and Separate Property, infra). Example 2: Assume the same facts as those in Example 1 except that Husband paid for the boat, which cost $25,000, with $10,000 that he inherited from his uncle and $15,000 in community property funds. The boat was not titled. The boat is two-fifths separate property ($10,000/$25,000) and three-fifths community property ($15,000). Pro-rata allocation does not apply when the special community property presumption (see IV.A.2.c., infra) applies. Instead, the spouse who contributes separate property to acquire community property is entitled to reimbursement (see IV.B.10.b. Separate property used to improve community property, infra).

All earnings during the marriage and all property acquired with those earnings are community property; all debts incurred during the marriage are the debts of the community. rules.canonical_name: General Requirements For A Valid Marriage In California
rules.rule_statement: Under California law, a valid marriage is a “personal relation arising out of a civil contract between two persons.” A marriage requires the consent and legal capacity of the two parties to enter into the contract of marriage. The age of consent to marry is 18 years of age. Any party under the age of consent must obtain a court order and written consent of a parent or guardian. In 2015, the U.S. Supreme Court recognized the right of same-sex couples to marry throughout the United States. However, consent alone does not constitute a marriage. To validate the marriage, the consent must be followed by formal legal procedures, including the issuance of a license, solemnization, and authentication.

In 2015, the U.S. Supreme Court recognized the right of same-sex couples to marry throughout the United States. However, consent alone does not constitute a marriage. To validate the marriage, the consent must be followed by formal legal procedures, including the issuance of a license, solemnization, and authentication.

rules.rule_statement: Although California does not recognize common-law marriage, the state does recognize a common-law marriage validly created in another state under the Full Faith and Credit Clause of the Constitution. Keep in mind that a person may contract a marriage in any jurisdiction, even if the parties are not domiciled there. Therefore, a California resident may be able to prove that he entered into a common-law marriage while temporarily residing in another jurisdiction.

rules.rule_statement: A void marriage is invalid and null from its inception. In other words, courts treat a void marriage as if it never legally existed. A voidable marriage is valid for all civil purposes between the parties and against the world until the marriage is judged a nullity.

“Void” Versus “Voidable” Marriages
A void marriage is invalid and null from its inception. In other words, courts treat a void marriage as if it never legally existed. A voidable marriage is valid for all civil purposes between the parties and against the world until the marriage is judged a nullity.

rules.rule_statement: Over time, a voidable marriage may become valid (i.e., non-voidable) if a proceeding to annul the voidable marriage is not commenced within the statutorily prescribed time limits (i.e., within four years after arriving at the age of consent or within four years after the date of the marriage). Once the applicable statutory period expires, a judicial termination of marital status and adjudication of the bundle of rights and responsibilities incident thereto must proceed by an ordinary dissolution.

Validation Of A Voidable Marriage
Over time, a voidable marriage may become valid (i.e., non-voidable) if a proceeding to annul the voidable marriage is not commenced within the statutorily prescribed time limits (i.e., within four years after arriving at the age of consent or within four years after the date of the marriage). Once the applicable statutory period expires, a judicial termination of marital status and adjudication of the bundle of rights and responsibilities incident thereto must proceed by an ordinary dissolution.

rules.rule_statement: A marriage between parents and children, ancestors and descendants of every degree, brothers and sisters (of half or whole blood), uncles and nieces, or aunts and nephews is incestuous and void from the beginning, “whether the relationship is legitimate or illegitimate.” A subsequent marriage is illegal and void from the beginning if either party had a spouse still living at the time the marriage was contracted, unless the former marriage was dissolved or adjudged a nullity before the date of the subsequent marriage. A subsequent marriage in which a party has a spouse still living is only voidable (valid until declared null) when, at the time of the subsequent marriage or domestic partnership, the former spouse: i) Has been absent and not known to be living for five successive years immediately preceding the subsequent marriage; or ii) Is “generally reputed” or believed to be dead. A marriage ostensibly contracted in accordance with California law is invalid from its inception and therefore void if the parties failed to comply with the requirements for a valid marriage or for a valid domestic partnership.

rules.rule_statement: A marriage is voidable and may be adjudged a nullity if any of the following conditions existed at the time of the marriage: One of the parties is under the age of lawful consent (18 years in California) and did not obtain the requisite parental or court consent unless, after attaining age 18, the party freely Underage cohabited with the other as her spouse. Either party was legally married to another at the time of the marriage, but the subsequent marriage is not void because the former spouse has been absent and not known to be living for five successive years immediately preceding the Prior existing marriage subsequent marriage, or is reputed to be dead. See II.A.3.b.3) Bigamy exception, supra. Either party was of “unsound mind” unless, “after coming to reason,” the afflicted party freely cohabited with the other as Unsound mind his spouse. Either party’s consent to the marriage was obtained by “force,” Force unless the parties thereafter freely cohabited as spouses. Either party was “physically incapable” of entering into the marital state, and the incapacity continues and appears to be Physical incapacity “incurable.” Either party’s consent to the marriage was obtained by “fraud,” unless the defrauded party afterward, and with full knowledge of the facts constituting the fraud, “freely” cohabited with the Fraud other party as spouses. EXAM NOTE: To support a nullity judgment, the fraud asserted must affect the very essence of the marriage.  Therefore, fraud or deceit sufﬁcient to avoid an ordinary contract will not necessarily warrant a nullity judgment.  Instead, the alleged misrepresentation or concealment must have been “vital to the relationship” directly affecting the purpose of the deceived party in consenting to the marriage. Example: Fraud was found when the party entered into the marriage to obtain U.S. residency status and never intended to assume marital relations. However, there was no fraud when the spouse concealed a drinking problem or misrepresented himself as wealthy or of good moral character. Cal. Fam. Code § 300. Cal. Fam. Code § 300 et seq. rules.rule_statement: In the California Supreme Court case of Marvin v. Marvin, the court held that the legislature’s definition of “marriage” did not preclude courts from granting a core set of marriage-like benefits to unmarried couples. The Marvin court held the following regarding unmarried cohabitants: i) Distribution of property acquired during a non-marital relationship is governed by general contract principles—not community property statutes; ii) Express contracts between non-marital partners will be enforced unless based on meretricious sexual services (i.e., prostitution); iii) If there is no express contract, the courts will examine the conduct of the parties to determine whether they had an implied contract; iv) Quantum meruit and other equitable remedies may be available for non-marital partners; and Cohabitants are not eligible to receive their partner’s benefits (e.g., social security) and, therefore, have no claim against the government or third parties for those benefits upon the partner’s disability or death. Even if the cohabitants later marry, contract principles will apply to property acquired during their prior cohabitation, and community property law will apply only to property they acquired during the marriage. Marvin v. Marvin, 557 P.2d 106 (Cal. 1976). See, e.g., In re Marriage of Tejeda, 102 Cal. Rptr. 3d 361 (Cal. Ct. App. 2009).

rules.rule_statement: A putative spouse is one who believed in good faith that a void or voidable marriage was valid. The purpose of the putative spouse doctrine is to protect the “innocent” party of an invalid marriage from losing community property rights. The good-faith inquiry is a subjective one that focuses on the actual state of mind of the alleged putative spouse. In determining good faith, the trial court must consider the totality of the circumstances, including the efforts made to create a valid marriage, the alleged putative spouse’s personal background and experience, and all the circumstances surrounding the marriage. Although the claimed belief need not pass a reasonable person test, the reasonableness or unreasonableness of one’s belief in the face of objective circumstances pointing to a marriage’s invalidity is a factor properly considered as part of the totality of the circumstances in determining whether the belief was genuinely and honestly held.

Putative Spouse Doctrine
A putative spouse is one who believed in good faith that a void or voidable marriage was valid. The purpose of the putative spouse doctrine is to protect the “innocent” party of an invalid marriage from losing community property rights. The good-faith inquiry is a subjective one that focuses on the actual state of mind of the alleged putative spouse. In determining good faith, the trial court must consider the totality of the circumstances, including the efforts made to create a valid marriage, the alleged putative spouse’s personal background and experience, and all the circumstances surrounding the marriage. Although the claimed belief need not pass a reasonable person test, the reasonableness or unreasonableness of one’s belief in the face of objective circumstances pointing to a marriage’s invalidity is a factor properly considered as part of the totality of the circumstances in determining whether the belief was genuinely and honestly held.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A putative spouse may rely on quasi-marital property principles to divide property, but property rights as a putative spouse stop accruing when the putative spouse discovers that the marriage is invalid. When both partners know the marriage is invalid, one partner can estop the other from denying the validity of the marriage if both parties are still engaged in the benefits of cohabitation. When only one spouse knows the marriage is invalid, some courts treat the knowing spouse like the unknowing spouse, despite the knowing spouse’s lack of good faith. In those jurisdictions, the knowing spouse may still obtain an ownership interest in the unknowing spouse’s quasi-marital property.

rules.rule_statement: All property that would have been considered community or quasi-community property had the marriage been valid is labeled “quasi-marital” property (“QMP”) in a putative marriage. “Quasi-marital” property is treated as community or quasi-community property. Therefore, at annulment, the putative spouse has a one?half interest in all quasi-marital property. Only a party who is determined to be a putative spouse can demand that the court divide property acquired during the marriage that would have been community property or quasi-community property if the marriage had not been void or voidable.

“Quasi-Marital” Property
All property that would have been considered community or quasi-community property had the marriage been valid is labeled “quasi-marital” property (“QMP”) in a putative marriage. “Quasi-marital” property is treated as community or quasi-community property. Therefore, at annulment, the putative spouse has a one?half interest in all quasi-marital property. Only a party who is determined to be a putative spouse can demand that the court divide property acquired during the marriage that would have been community property or quasi-community property if the marriage had not been void or voidable.

rules.canonical_name: When A Decedent Has Both A Legal Spouse And A Putative Spouse
rules.rule_statement: If a decedent has two “spouses”—one to whom the decedent is legally married and the other who is considered a putative spouse—then the estate is split equally. Example: Husband married Wife 1 in 1990 and had three children with her. Husband then married Wife 2 in 2000 and raised four children with her. Wife 1 was considered Husband’s legal spouse, and Husband’s marriage to Wife 2 was void because it was a bigamous marriage. If Wife 2 had a good-faith belief that she was Husband’s wife, then Husband’s estate would be split equally between Wife 1 and Wife 2, “presumably on the theory that innocent wives of practicing bigamists are entitled to equal shares of property accumulated during the active phase of the bigamy.” Ceja v. When A Decedent Has Both A Legal Spouse And A Putative Spouse
If a decedent has two “spouses”—one to whom the decedent is legally married and the other who is considered a putative spouse—then the estate is split equally. Example: Husband married Wife 1 in 1990 and had three children with her. Husband then married Wife 2 in 2000 and raised four children with her. Wife 1 was considered Husband’s legal spouse, and Husband’s marriage to Wife 2 was void because it was a bigamous marriage. rules.rule_statement: The rules regarding the death or divorce of married couples also apply to the death or dissolution of domestic partnerships, if registered under California’s domestic partnership registration laws. Domestic partners are two adults who (1) share one another’s lives in an intimate and committed relationship of mutual caring (cohabitation is not required) and (2) would otherwise have the capacity to marry. On January 1, 2005, the California Domestic Partner Rights and Responsibilities Act became effective, marking the first time that community property laws applied to domestic partners. Note: The law applies retroactively to all previously registered domestic partners.

rules.rule_statement: An agreement is unconscionable if, when it was executed, all of the following applied to the party against whom enforcement is sought: i) She was not provided a fair, reasonable, and full disclosure of the property or financial obligations of the other party; ii) She did not voluntarily and expressly waive, in writing, any right to disclosure of the property or financial obligations of the other party beyond the disclosure provided; and iii) She did not have, or reasonably could not have had, adequate knowledge of the property or financial obligations of the other party. EXAM NOTE: Pay attention to facts suggesting that the spouses’ circumstances have changed signiﬁcantly over time.  A premarital agreement must have been unconscionable “when it was executed” to be invalidated on the grounds of unconscionability.  A premarital agreement is not unenforceable merely because subsequent circumstances make a previously executed premarital agreement seem unconscionable.

rules.canonical_name: Spousal Support In Agreements As Of 2002
rules.rule_statement: Premarital agreements made on or after January 1, 2002, are controlled by amendments to the California version of the Uniform Premarital Agreement Act. Any provision in a premarital agreement regarding spousal support will not be enforceable if: i) The party against whom enforcement is sought was not represented by independent counsel at the time that the premarital agreement was signed; ii) The provision regarding spousal support is unconscionable at the time of enforcement—even if the party against whom enforcement is sought was represented by independent counsel. An otherwise unenforceable provision in a premarital agreement regarding spousal support will not become enforceable solely because the party against whom enforcement is sought was represented by independent counsel. Cal. Fam. Code § 1500. Cal. Fam. Code § 1601. Cal. Fam. Code § 1611. Cal. Fam. Code § 1612. Cal. Fam. Code § 1612(b). See, e.g., In re. Marriage of Bellio, 129 Cal. Rptr. 2d 556 (Cal. Ct. App. 2003). Cal. Fam. Code § 1615. Cal. Fam. Code § 1615(c). Cal. Fam. Code § 1615(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: Until January 1, 1985, oral agreements were sufficient to transmute property if there was evidence supporting the transmutation, such as an express agreement or an agreement implied in fact from either party’s behavior. Example: In In re Marriage of Jafeman, 105 Cal. Rptr. 483 (Cal. Ct. App. 1972), the only evidence supporting an agreement to convert the husband’s house that he owned before marriage to community property was his wife’s testimony that “they always referred to the residence as ‘our house,’” and her belief that the home was ‘our property.’” The court held that referring to the home as “our home” did not transmute the property because the evidence failed to show the husband intended to transmute his separate property into community property.

The legislature intended that the rules regarding transmutation apply to all property owned by spouses, regardless of when they acquired the property.

However, this requirement does not apply to cases in which one or both spouses purchase property from a third party and specify a particular form of ownership. rules.rule_statement: Rule There is a general presumption that all property acquired during marriage is community property, which is rebuttable with a preponderance of evidence to the contrary. The general community property presumption can be rebutted in various ways, including written title evidencing that a gift was intended, evidence that the property was acquired by gift, bequest, devise, or descent or after permanent separation, tracing the funds used to purchase the asset to a separate-property source (see I.G. Tracing, supra), or with an agreement designating property as separate property.

rules.rule_statement: Under the exhaustion method, the separate-property proponent must prove that community property funds in the account were already exhausted by the payment of family expenses at the time that the asset was purchased. Family expenses include food, clothing, housing, and recreation. In addition, certain presumptions apply to family expenses when they are paid from a commingled account, which presumptions must be factored in before applying the exhaustion method of tracing: i) Community-property funds are presumed to be used when paying family expenses, and separate-property funds are presumed to be used only when community-property funds have been exhausted; and ii) Any separate-property funds used to pay for family expenses are presumed to be a gift to the community.

rules.canonical_name: Goodwill Of A Business Or Professional Practice
rules.rule_statement: Goodwill is the intangible value that most businesses have, based on the expectation of future business and an established name or reputation. Goodwill generates income beyond the labor of the spouse and beyond a reasonable return on capital and physical assets. The business subject to goodwill must be an entity as opposed to a natural person. The goodwill of a business is transferable property. To the extent that a business or professional practice has been developed by a spouse during marriage, there is a community property interest in the goodwill subject to division upon dissolution. Courts use two valuation techniques to calculate the value of goodwill: i) Determine the market sales valuation of the goodwill (i.e., the price for which the goodwill could be sold, often established by bona fide offers to purchase the business or goodwill) through expert testimony; or ii) Compare the past excess earnings of the business or professional practice to the typical or average earnings of peer businesses or practices to determine what earnings are attributable to the goodwill. When a covenant not to compete is part of the sale of business goodwill, the “value” of the covenant not to compete will be deducted from the value of the community property because the covenant restricts the business spouse’s post-divorce labor. There is no adjustment when the sale is hypothetical rather than actual.

rules.rule_statement: On divorce or legal separation, when a spouse has earned an educational degree or a professional license, the community estate is entitled to be reimbursed for contributions to the costs of acquiring the degree or license. These costs are normally limited to direct educational or training expenses, such as tuition, lab fees, and textbook costs, and do not include living expenses. The amount reimbursed includes interest at the legal rate, accruing from the end of the calendar year in which the community contributions were made. The community estate will be reimbursed only if: ii) The earning capacity of the educated spouse was substantially improved; and iii) The parties did not waive the right of reimbursement in writing. This right of reimbursement is in lieu of any right of the other spouse to a percentage of the enhanced earning ability of the spouse who acquired the degree or license.

rules.rule_statement: Retirement benefits are typically provided under either a defined-contribution plan or a defined-benefit plan. Defined-contribution plans do not guarantee a specific benefit to be paid to an employee upon maturity of the employee’s right to the benefit (e.g., upon retirement), but instead provide for a specific contribution to be made by the employer, the employee, or both on a regular basis to an account. Under a defined-benefit plan (i.e., pension), an employee is entitled to a specific periodic monetary benefit upon retirement for the remainder of the employee’s life. Expert testimony is not required to determine the current value of a defined-contribution plan. Defined-benefit plans are typically based on the employee’s average salary over a specific period. An employee is also usually required to reach a specific age to collect a pension (e.g., 65 years of age). Rule Retirement benefits earned during marriage are treated as community property under California law, regardless of when the employee-spouse collects the value of the benefit. If the employee-spouse also earned benefits before or after the marriage, the court will need to determine how to allocate the benefits between community and separate property. rules.rule_statement: The time rule applies to allocate the community and separate property portions of a pension earned both before and during marriage. The community- property percentage is calculated by dividing the years during which the spouse was employed during the marriage by the total number of years that the employed spouse has been participating in the retirement plan. The remaining percentage is the employed spouse’s separate property.

rules.rule_statement: If an employee spouse is not yet eligible for retirement upon divorce, a court may reserve jurisdiction to effect a division in kind. Under a division in kind, the court orders that when the employed spouse retires, the other spouse will receive a percentage of each pension check using the “time rule” method. The result of that division is the community-property percentage of the retirement plan.

The result of that division is the community-property percentage of the retirement plan.

rules.rule_statement: If an employee spouse is not yet eligible for retirement upon divorce, the other method of dealing with pension plans involves obtaining “actuarial evaluation” of a retirement plan by a qualified actuary to determine its present cash value. Through this process, the non-participant spouse in the retirement plan is cashed out upon divorce and awarded assets of equal value to the pension benefits. Example: If the husband’s retirement plan provides that he will receive $500 per month upon his retirement at age 62, and the husband is presently 37 years old, the actuary estimates how much money would have to be deposited in an interest-bearing account to yield interest income in 25 years of $500 per month. This process includes an estimation of the long-range interest rates that would be in effect over that period of time. With a cash-out, the employed spouse receives his retirement plan, and other community property assets are awarded to his spouse to result in an overall equal division of community property.

rules.canonical_name: Refusal To Retire By Pensionable Spouse
rules.rule_statement: If the employed spouse is eligible to retire but chooses not to, the other spouse may assert his right to receive the retirement benefits due him from the employed spouse’s retirement pension. The court may order a private employer’s retirement plan to pay the non-employee spouse his share of the employed spouse’s retirement benefit. The employed spouse must receive notice of the demand.

rules.rule_statement: Stock options that are granted during the marriage and which vest (i.e., become exercisable) during the marriage are community property. Stock options that are granted during the marriage but which vest after the date of separation are part community property and part separate property. The characterization of these stock options depends on the type of compensation they are designated to replace. Courts will determine the community-property share of the stock options by applying one of two formulas, depending on whether the stock options were granted as a reward for past service or as an incentive for future service.

rules.source_text:
Stock Options
Stock options that are granted during the marriage and which vest (i.e., become exercisable) during the marriage are community property. Stock options that are granted during the marriage but which vest after the date of separation are part community property and part separate property. The characterization of these stock options depends on the type of compensation they are designated to replace. Courts will determine the community-property share of the stock options by applying one of two formulas, depending on whether the stock options were granted as a reward for past service or as an incentive for future service.
topic_path: Characterization Of Special Types Of Property

rules.rule_statement: Rule The Hug formula applies if the stock options are granted to compensate an employee for past service (or to attract a new employee). To apply the Hug formula, the court will calculate the amount of time between the date of hire (“DOH”) and date of separation (“DOS”). That number will be divided by the amount of time between the DOH and the date the options vest (“DOV”). The result is the community-property share of the stock options. Rule The Hug formula can be written as follows: CP = (DOH – DOS) / (DOH – DOV) × # of shares SP = # of shares – CP Example: A and B were married in 2005. In 2012, B was hired by a new employer. In 2014, the employer granted B 100 stock options (“SO”), which vest in 2018. A and B permanently separated in 2016. Applying Hug: CP share = (2012 – 2016) / (2012 – 2018) × 100 SO = 4/6 × 100 SO = 67 SO SP share = 100 SO – 67 SO = 33 SO

Hug Formula
Rule The Hug formula applies if the stock options are granted to compensate an employee for past service (or to attract a new employee). To apply the Hug formula, the court will calculate the amount of time between the date of hire (“DOH”) and date of separation (“DOS”). That number will be divided by the amount of time between the DOH and the date the options vest (“DOV”). The result is the community-property share of the stock options. Rule The Hug formula can be written as follows: CP = (DOH – DOS) / (DOH – DOV) × # of shares SP = # of shares – CP Example: A and B were married in 2005. In 2012, B was hired by a new employer. In 2014, the employer granted B 100 stock options (“SO”), which vest in 2018. A and B permanently separated in 2016. Applying Hug: CP share = (2012 – 2016) / (2012 – 2018) × 100 SO = 4/6 × 100 SO = 67 SO SP share = 100 SO – 67 SO = 33 SO

rules.rule_statement: Rule The Nelson formula applies if the stock options are granted to incentivize continued employment. To apply the Nelson formula, the court will calculate the amount of time between the date of the grant (“DOG”) and the date of separation (“DOS”). That number will be divided by the amount of time between the DOG and the DOV. The result is the community-property share of the stock options. Rule The Nelson formula can be written as follows: CP = (DOG – DOS) / (DOG – DOV) × # of shares SP = # of shares – CP Example: Using the example above, applying Nelson: CP share = (2014 – 2016) / (2014 – 2018) × 100 SO = 2/4 × 100 SO = 50 SO SP share = 100 SO – 50 SO = 50 SO

rules.rule_statement: Disability pay encompasses payments made to an employee because of an inability to work because of some type of physical incapacity. California classifies workers’ compensation pay and disability pay in the same way. Therefore, this discussion of disability pay also applies to workers’ compensation payments. The characterization of disability pay depends on the type of compensation it is designated to replace. In particular, if disability pay is intended to replace marital earnings or retirement benefits, then it is characterized as community property. If it is intended to replace earnings after dissolution, then it is characterized as separate property.

rules.source_text:
Disability Pay
Disability pay encompasses payments made to an employee because of an inability to work because of some type of physical incapacity. California classifies workers’ compensation pay and disability pay in the same way. Therefore, this discussion of disability pay also applies to workers’ compensation payments. The characterization of disability pay depends on the type of compensation it is designated to replace. In particular, if disability pay is intended to replace marital earnings or retirement benefits, then it is characterized as community property. If it is intended to replace earnings after dissolution, then it is characterized as separate property.

rules.rule_statement: When an employee is terminated, he receives severance pay to compensate for lost earnings while the employee finds another source of employment. Courts are divided on how to characterize severance pay. But if it is structured similarly to retirement benefits, then it is community property. Severance that was meant to replace lost earnings after divorce or legal separation is separate property. This controversy exists, in part, because an employer is under no contractual duty to provide severance pay.

rules.rule_statement: Whole life insurance is a type of insurance policy that has both an insurance component and an investment component. At death or divorce, a court will characterize the proceeds as community property in proportion to the number of premium payments that were made with community funds. If the policy was paid only with community funds, a surviving spouse gets one-half of the proceeds. And the other half goes to the named third-party beneficiary, if other than the surviving spouse.

rules.rule_statement: Generally, income from a separate-property business is characterized as separate property. However, if a spouse’s labor enhances the value of the separate- property business during the marriage, then the community estate is entitled to reimbursement for that value. To achieve substantial justice between the parties, a court has discretion to apply the most appropriate and equitable approach to calculate the community- property and separate-property shares. Most often, the court will choose between the Pereira approach and the Van Camp approach. Rule The Pereira approach is used if the increase in value is primarily due to the personal skills and effort of the managing spouse. The Van Camp approach is used when the primary reason for the increase in value is the character of the separate property itself, rather than the labor of the spouse. The court is also free to apply a hybrid Pereria-Van Camp approach when appropriate in a given case to achieve “substantial justice” between the parties.

rules.rule_statement: The Pereira approach assumes that a spouse’s personal time, effort, character, energy, ability, and capacity caused an increase in the value of the separate-property business during the marriage. In effect, the Pereira approach favors the community property portion of the estate. The court will determine how much of the increase in the value of the business was due to normal capital appreciation of the initial separate-property investment, giving a fair rate of return based on the legal rate of simple interest, and allocate any excess to the community. SP = Value of the SP Business at the Time of Marriage + (Value of the SP Business at the Time of Marriage × Fair Rate of Return × Years of Marriage) CP = Fair Market Value (“FMV”) of the Business at Divorce – SP EXAM NOTE: For the bar examination essays, use 10% as the fair rate of return. Example: H purchased a business for $100,000, two years before marrying W. H received a salary of $40,000 per year, even though the court has determined that the reasonable value of his services is $80,000 per year. Community expenses paid from the business account were $50,000 per year. H and W seek a divorce 10 years later, and the appraised value of the business is $1,000,000. Applying Pereira: SP share = $100,000 + ($100,000 × 10% × 10) = $200,000 CP share = $1,000,000 – $200,000 = $800,000

rules.rule_statement: The Van Camp approach allocates to community property the reasonable value of the spouse’s efforts (i.e., the community income), less family expenses paid from the earning. The remainder goes to separate property. In effect, the Van Camp approach favors the separate-property portion of the separate property business. The Van Camp approach can be broken into two steps: First, the court applies the Van Camp formula to calculate the value of the community portion of the business. Second, the court determines the community property and separate property portions of the separate property business, then uses these figures to decide if the community has been compensated for its share of the value of the business by a salary or other distributions. If the community estate has already received the value calculated in step one, neither the community estate nor the other spouse will receive a money judgment against the business upon divorce. If the estate has not been compensated, the court may award an equitable distribution to the community estate. If the spouse’s efforts were compensated by a high salary or large bonuses, then the court is likely to find that the community estate has already been fairly compensated. EXAM NOTE: Application of the Van Camp approach, unlike the Pereria approach, does not require a ﬁnding that the business appreciated in market value. Courts use the Van Camp formula to calculate the community-property value that has been added to the business after family expenses are deducted. The court estimates the reasonable (market rate) salary for the working spouse’s services, then subtracts from that the family’s annual expenses that are presumed to be paid from community property. The remainder is classified as separate property. CP = (Reasonable Value of Services – Annual Family Expenses) × Years of Marriage SP = FMV of the Business at Divorce – CP If the reasonable value of the spouse’s services does not exceed the family’s deducted living expenses, no community share remains. Some lower courts have applied a “total recapitulation” approach to the Van Camp formula, suggesting that family living expenses during the marriage may be charged to the community income of the business even when the spouse received no salary or distributions from the business. Although no California appellate court has ruled on the issue directly, dicta suggests that this approach is inappropriate. EXAM NOTE: On the exam, only deduct annual family expenditures that could potentially be traced to the business (e.g., salary received up to the family’s cost of living, expenditure reimbursements, living expenses paid from a business account). The Van Camp formula does not determine what value the community estate should receive upon divorce. Instead, it determines how much CP value the spouse is presumed to have added to the business. rules.rule_statement: To determine whether the community estate or the other spouse should receive a distribution from the business upon divorce, a second step is often required. If the sum of the funds distributed from the business to the spouse (i.e., salary, expense reimbursements, family expenses paid from business funds, etc.) meets or exceeds the fair market rate value of the spouse’s services, the court assumes that the community has been adequately compensated for the spouse’s labor. In these cases, the entire business is treated as separate property, and the analysis ends with no judgment against the value of the business. Example: Using the example above, H received a $40,000 annual salary for 10 years, resulting in a total of $400,000 in salary that has already been received by the community estate ($40,000 x 10 years = $400,000). Because the added CP value calculated under the Van Camp formula was only $300,000, the community estate has already been adequately compensated. The business will be deemed SP, and neither the community estate nor W will receive a judgment against it. If the spouse did not receive the fair value of her services, the court may, in its discretion, award the difference between the reasonable value of the services provided and the amount actually received by the community estate. Alternatively, the court may order payment of the other spouse’s half of the difference after the division of marital property. This award is called an equitable apportionment. Example: Using the example above, assume that H received a $10,000 salary when the reasonable value of his service was $80,000 per year, but that H still withdrew $50,000 a year for family expenses. Equitable apportionment may be available because the total salary received from the business during the marriage ($10,000 x 10 years = $100,000) is less than the CP value of the business under the Van Camp formula (($80,000 – $50,000) × 10 = $300,000). As long as all family expenses were drawn directly from the business assets, not paid out of salary, the easiest way to calculate the proper equitable apportionment owed to the community estate is to find the difference between the CP value calculated by the Van Camp formula and the total salary received by the spouse during the marriage. The court may either make this allocation to the community estate, or order the spouse holding the separate property in the business to pay the other spouse their CP share of the difference after the division of marital property. Example: Using the example above, because no family expenses were paid out of the salary, the community estate is owed: $300,000 (business’s CP value) – $100,000 (total salary received) = $200,000. The court may allocate this to the community estate directly or order H to pay W $100,000 ($200,000 x W’s ½ CP share) after the marital property is divided. Be aware that any time family expenses are paid out of the salary received by the spouse, proper equitable apportionment requires a different approach. The goal in these situations is to determine the difference between the reasonable value of a spouse’s service and the value actually received by the community estate during the marriage. There are multiple valid formulas that can be used in these situations, but we recommend one of the following because they can be applied to all situations:

rules.rule_statement: Rule Courts determine the character of property acquired on credit based on the lender’s intent to rely on (1) the purchaser’s separate property or (2) a community asset or income stream. In the absence of evidence proving that the seller “primarily relied” on the purchaser’s separate property in extending credit, courts generally apply the presumption that property acquired during the marriage is community property. Additionally, proceeds of a loan made on a spouse’s personal credit is community property. Example (1): H and W obtained an unsecured loan before they were married. They later separated and must determine the character of the loan proceeds. The lender is deemed to have relied on the personal credit and community earnings of H and W, so the loan proceeds are community property. The signatures on the loan are some evidence of the lender’s intent to rely on their personal credit, but they are not conclusive. Example (2): H and W obtained a loan secured by a deed of trust on W’s separate property. The loan proceeds are deemed to be W’s separate property.

rules.canonical_name: Separate Property Used To Improve The Other Spouse’S Separate Property
rules.rule_statement: Before 2005, when a spouse used her separate property to improve the other spouse’s separate property, the contribution was presumed to be a gift, and she was not entitled to reimbursement. After 2005, she has a statutory right to be reimbursed for the contribution unless there has been a transmutation in writing or a written waiver of the right to reimbursement. This right also exists for other contributions of her separate property to the acquisition of the other spouse’s separate property, such as down payments and payments that reduce the principal of a loan used to finance the purchase or improvement of the property. But this does not include payments of interest on the loan or payments made for maintenance, insurance, or taxation of the property. The amount reimbursed is without interest or any adjustment for change in monetary values and it may not exceed the net value of the property at the time of the division.

rules.canonical_name: Community Property Used To Improve A Spouse’S Own Separate Property
rules.rule_statement: When a spouse uses community property to improve his own separate property, the community is entitled to either reimbursement for the funds expended or the enhanced value of the property, whichever is greater. Before 1975, if the wife used community property to improve her own separate property, the improvement was presumed a gift because only the husband had management and control over the community property. The court presumed the husband’s donative intent in the wife’s use of community property. If the husband used community property to improve his own separate property without the wife’s consent, the community was entitled to reimbursement to avoid constructive fraud against his wife.

rules.rule_statement: If the injury is caused by a third-party tortfeasor before the marriage, then the tort recovery is separate property. The injured spouse must reimburse the community if community property is used to pay, or the other spouse if that spouse’s separate property is used to pay any expenses attributable to the injury. When the injury is caused by a third-party tortfeasor during the marriage, the tort recovery is characterized as community property. The court will award any tort recovery to the injured spouse at divorce if it has not been spent or commingled with community-property funds, unless the court finds that the interests of justice warrant awarding some of the recovery to the non-injured spouse. However, the court may not order more than one-half of the recovery to the non-injured spouse. If the damages have been commingled with community property, then they lose their statutory protection and become part of the community.

rules.rule_statement: A married person is personally liable for debt incurred for the “necessaries of life” (living expenses) before the date of separation. Personal liability means that all of the person’s property—both community and separate—is liable for the other spouse’s debts. “Necessaries of life” are defined as living costs consistent with the spouses’ station in life, including food, clothing, shelter, and medical expenses. Absent a written waiver, the non-debtor spouse can be reimbursed for any separate-property funds used, if community property or separate property of the debtor spouse was available at the time the debt was paid. Upon divorce, the non-debtor spouse remains personally liable only if it is assigned to him by the court.

“Necessaries Of Life”—Before Separation
A married person is personally liable for debt incurred for the “necessaries of life” (living expenses) before the date of separation. Personal liability means that all of the person’s property—both community and separate—is liable for the other spouse’s debts. “Necessaries of life” are defined as living costs consistent with the spouses’ station in life, including food, clothing, shelter, and medical expenses. Absent a written waiver, the non-debtor spouse can be reimbursed for any separate-property funds used, if community property or separate property of the debtor spouse was available at the time the debt was paid. Upon divorce, the non-debtor spouse remains personally liable only if it is assigned to him by the court.

Preexisting child and spousal support, above), and tort obligations (see D. Tort obligations, above). When this right exists and it has not been waived in writing, it does not matter which spouse used the property to satisfy a debt or whether it was voluntary. The reimbursement right must be asserted in divorce proceedings involving the division of community or quasi-community property or in proceedings upon the death of a spouse or, if earlier, within three years from the date that the spouse who is exercising the right has knowledge of the debt satisfaction. rules.rule_statement: Rule California courts prohibit gifts or the disposal of community personal property for less than fair and reasonable value unless the other spouse has given written consent. Equitable defenses of laches, estoppel, unjust enrichment, waiver, or ratification may apply. During the marriage, if one spouse improperly gifted or disposed of community personal property for less than fair and reasonable value without the other spouse’s written consent, then the non-consenting spouse has the right to either (1) ratify the gift or (2) revoke the gift and sue to recover all of the property (recapture). This rule applies only to gifts to third parties. Gifts between spouses are governed by the transmutation statute, which allows a spouse to transmute separate property into community property, community property into separate property, and one spouse’s separate property into the other spouse’s separate property. After the death of the donor spouse, the non-consenting spouse has the right to (1) ratify the gift or (2) void the gift up to one-half of the value of the gift. A similar remedy is available for certain transfers of quasi-community property (see VI.C. Quasi-Community Property, supra).

Quasi-Community Property
Because the quasi-community property classification is only triggered when the marriage terminates upon death or divorce, the record owner (i.e., the person on the title) of a specific quasi-community asset has exclusive management and control over the asset during the marriage. This includes the right to transfer the property. However, the other spouse may be able to avoid the transfer of up to one-half of the value of the asset if: i) The transferor spouse transferred the asset to a third party without receiving substantial consideration and without the written consent or joinder of the other spouse; ii) The transferor spouse died domiciled in California; and iii) The transfer allows the transferor spouse to collect income from or retain possession, control, or an interest (e.g., right of survivorship) in the property. In these cases, the non-consenting spouse may require the transferee to restore to the transferor spouse’s estate:

rules.canonical_name: One Half Of The Property If The Transferee Retains The Property
rules.rule_statement: ii) One-half of the property’s proceeds if the transferee does not retain the property; or iii) One-half of the property’s value at the time of transfer if the transferee retains neither the property nor its proceeds. All property consequently restored to the decedent’s estate belongs to the surviving spouse as though the transfer had not been made. Cal. Prob. Code § 102(a). Cal. Prob. Code § 102(c).

rules.canonical_name: Remedies For A Managing Spouse’S Breach Of Duty
rules.rule_statement: Finding a breach of fiduciary duty requires proof of deliberate misappropriation or grossly negligent or reckless conduct. Mere incompetence or ordinary negligence is not actionable. A non-managing spouse has a claim against the managing spouse for a breach of the fiduciary duty that results in the impairment of the non-managing spouse’s present undivided one-half interest in the community estate. Remedies include the right to an accounting, adding a name onto title, a greater share of the community property, and attorney’s fees and costs. The action must be brought within three years from the date that the spouse bringing the action had knowledge of the breach, or otherwise upon legal separation, divorce or upon a spouse’s death. If a spouse intentionally damages community property, or property in which the other spouse had an interest, then he may be held criminally liable. A spouse who fraudulently conceals assets can be subject to a penalty amounting to 100% of the concealed asset. Cal. Fam. Code § 1100(e); 721(b). Cal. Fam. Code § 1100(e). Cal. Fam. Code § 1101(a). Cal. Fam. Code § 1101(h); In re Marriage of Rossi, 108 Cal. Rptr. 2d 270 (Cal. Ct. App. 2001).

rules.rule_statement: When property is put in joint title, it is treated completely differently from property that is put in only one spouse’s name. Under the special title presumption, ownership of property is determined by the title of the property upon the death of the title holder(s). Therefore, if the title indicates that the spouses hold property as joint tenants, each spouse holds an undivided half separate-property interest in the property for the duration of the marriage. However, as of 1987, all jointly titled property (e.g., joint tenancies, tenancies in common, etc.) acquired during marriage is presumed to be community property upon divorce. When the husband and wife make an agreement to hold the property jointly, the presumption that arises can be rebutted only by another agreement that the property is not held jointly (e.g., in writing or in a clear statement in the deed or title). See Cal. Evid. Code § 662; Cal. Fam. Code § 2581(a). See Davis v. Davis, 35 Cal. Rptr. 281 (Cal. Ct. App. 1963). Cal. Fam. Code §§ 2580(c), 2581.

rules.canonical_name: Community Property With Right Of Survivorship
rules.rule_statement: As of July 1, 2001, a husband and wife may hold title as community property with right of survivorship. This means that their property will be treated as community property during marriage and at divorce. But if a spouse dies before a judgment terminates the marriage, the surviving spouse will own the property through the right of survivorship (e.g., the property will be treated like a joint tenancy). Community property with right of survivorship must be expressly declared in the deed or title. Note: Upon death or divorce, a couple’s joint tenancies and community property held with right of survivorship are generally distributed identically. Although you should be able to identify how each is created on the exam, the tax policy reasons that spouses might choose one over the other are beyond the scope of the bar exam. See Cal. Prob. Code § 5600 et. seq.

rules.rule_statement: The court in Lucas held that when property was purchased with separate-property and community-property funds, the property was presumptively community property, and the separate-property funds were deemed to be a gift to the community. Because the property was taken in joint form, at divorce the special community property presumption—limited to single-family residences—applied. The special community-property presumption could be rebutted by clear and convincing evidence that both spouses did not intend to hold the property as community property. Before 1984, the presumption could be rebutted with parol evidence of the spouses’ intent, including an oral or implied agreement.

Pre-1984: Lucas Ruling
The court in Lucas held that when property was purchased with separate-property and community-property funds, the property was presumptively community property, and the separate-property funds were deemed to be a gift to the community. Because the property was taken in joint form, at divorce the special community property presumption—limited to single-family residences—applied. The special community-property presumption could be rebutted by clear and convincing evidence that both spouses did not intend to hold the property as community property. Before 1984, the presumption could be rebutted with parol evidence of the spouses’ intent, including an oral or implied agreement.

rules.rule_statement: In response to the Lucas ruling, in 1984, the California legislature enacted legislation amending the special community-property presumption. The 1984 amendments made three important changes to the law: i) The special community-property presumption was expanded to include all joint tenancy property held by married couples; ii) The special community-property presumption could be rebutted only by an express statement in the deed or a collateral writing evidencing the spouses’ intent to hold the property as separate property; and iii) A spouse making a separate-property “contribution” had a right of reimbursement for that contribution, but without interest. In 1987, the statute was again amended to expand the application of the special community-property presumption to all jointly held property at divorce. If the property title was transferred to a joint form after marriage, it was “acquired during marriage” for purposes of this statute. Under the special community-property presumption, property held as joint tenancy, tenancy in common, and tenancy by the entirety is community property at divorce or legal separation. Note: The Anti-Lucas legislation applies only to divorce or legal separation, not death.

rules.canonical_name: Separate Property Contribution To Acquisition Of Community Property
rules.rule_statement: When separate property is used to make a down payment or other contribution to the acquisition of community property or quasi-community property, the presumption of community property controls. If this presumption is not rebutted, the separate property is limited to a right of reimbursement after tracing any payments to a separate-property source. Separate-property contributions include down payments, payments for improvements, and payments that reduce the principal of a loan used to finance purchase or improvement of the property. Payments toward interest on the loan, taxes, and insurance are excluded because they do not contribute to the equitable interest. In addition, the amount reimbursed is without interest or adjustment for change in monetary values and may not exceed the net value of the property at the time of the property division. If the presumption is rebutted by a writing preserving the separate-property interest in the property, the separate property will acquire a pro-rata interest in the property in the proportion that the down payment bears to the purchase price. The formula used to calculate the community-property and separate- property apportionment is a pro-rata calculation often referred to as the Aufmuth formula.

rules.canonical_name: Community Property Mortgage Payments On Separate Real Property
rules.rule_statement: Anti-Lucas legislation does not apply to community-property contributions for separate real property. When community property is used to make mortgage payments on separate property, the community acquires an interest in the property to the extent that the equity is enhanced by community-property payments to the principal. Payments toward interest, taxes, and insurance are excluded because they do not contribute to the equitable interest. The community property will acquire a pro-rata interest in the property in the proportion that the community-property payments bear to the total purchase price, including any appreciation in value during the time of community-property payments. The formula used to calculate the community-property and separate-property apportionment is a pro-rata calculation often referred to as the Moore-Marsden Formula. EXAM NOTE: On the bar exam, you will not need to calculate a complicated formula because the necessary facts will be purposely absent. Determine whether the general or a special presumption applies.  Explain the basic concept of pro-rata apportionment of the appreciated value of the property, and how it is based on the amount of buy-in from payments made toward the principal (excluding payments of interest, taxes and insurance). In re Marriage of Lucas, 614 P.2d 285 (Cal. 1980). See Cal. Fam. Code §§ 2640, 2851. Cal. Fam. Code § 2581. In re Marriage of Heikes, 899 P.2d 1349 (Cal. 1995). In re Marriage of Hilke, 841 P.2d 891 (Cal. 1992). Cal. Fam. Code § 2640(a). Cal. Fam. Code § 2640(a), (b). In re Marriage of Aufmuth, 52 Cal. Rptr. 668 (Cal. Ct. App. 1979). In re Marriage of Moore, 618 P.2d 208 (Cal. 1980). In re Marriage of Marsden, 181 Cal. Rptr. 910 (Cal. Ct. App. 1982).

rules.rule_statement: California is a no-fault state. The grounds for divorce are irreconcilable differences and incurable insanity.
rules.short_rule_statement: California is a no-fault state. The grounds for divorce are irreconcilable differences and incurable insanity.
rules.source_text:
Division Of Property At Divorce
California is a no-fault state. The grounds for divorce are irreconcilable differences and incurable insanity.

rules.rule_statement: Rule California requires an equal division of the community property estate at divorce, absent a written agreement to the contrary. There is a mandatory division of community property, except in instances of wrongdoing, breach of fiduciary duty, or fraud. The joint liabilities and obligations of the parties are subtracted from the total fair market value of the community assets, yielding the net community estate. The general California distributional structure is to divide each community asset equally, but courts have broad discretion to use an aggregate theory of distribution when economic circumstances warrant. Under this approach each spouse will receive one-half of the net community estate, but not necessarily one-half of each item of property. Cal. Fam. Code § 2550. Cal. Fam. Code § 2601.

rules.rule_statement: At divorce, the debts that are unpaid at the time of trial must be divided equally or assigned to one of the spouses. A debt incurred before the marriage “shall be confirmed without offset to the spouse who incurred the debt.” A debt incurred during the marriage but before separation can be characterized as a separate property liability if the debt was not incurred for the benefit of the community. The exceptions are that educational loans and tort liability are not treated as other debts incurred during the marriage.

The exceptions are that educational loans and tort liability are not treated as other debts incurred during the marriage.

rules.rule_statement: Federal law preempts community property law regarding distribution of some federal benefits. The community does not acquire an interest in benefits before they are paid to a spouse, but once paid, the asset is subject to state community property rules regarding commingling or transmutation of separate property to community property. Examples of federal benefits subject to preemption include Social Security, military life insurance, ERISA, and federal disability benefits. Railroad retirement benefits are not preempted by federal law and are subject to community property characterization. Courts differ as to military retirement benefits, though a portion of the payments are typically allowed to be characterized as community property after various deductions from the gross retirement pay. Hisquierdo v. Hisquierdo, 439 U.S. 572 (1979).

Railroad retirement benefits are not preempted by federal law and are subject to community property characterization. Courts differ as to military retirement benefits, though a portion of the payments are typically allowed to be characterized as community property after various deductions from the gross retirement pay. Hisquierdo v. Hisquierdo, 439 U.S. 572 (1979).

rules.rule_statement: Property transferred between a husband and wife during divorce is not a taxable event. The court must equally allocate any tax liability incurred from the sale of an asset as a direct result of the divorce proceedings. Property not distributed during the divorce proceedings is subject to future litigation because it was not adjudicated (e.g., claim for pension division). There is some division in the courts of appeal regarding post-judgment motions to distribute an omitted asset because the court generally integrates the separation agreement into the divorce decree, and the agreement is usually considered the final settlement.

rules.rule_statement: At death, the character of property is presumptively as expressed in the title, under the special title presumption, meaning that a joint tenancy property is presumed to be a joint tenancy, and community property is presumed to be community property. The quasi-community property is distributed in the same manner as community property. However, real property located outside California is subject to the intestacy laws of the jurisdiction where the property is located. EXAM NOTE: Compare this rule with the presumption that applies to the dissolution of marriage or legal separation—property acquired during the marriage in joint form is presumed to be community property. Cal. Prob. Code § 66; Cal. Fam. Code § 125.

The quasi-community property is distributed in the same manner as community property. However, real property located outside California is subject to the intestacy laws of the jurisdiction where the property is located. rules.rule_statement: Life insurance premiums paid with community funds make the policy community property. Therefore, if a decedent names a beneficiary other than his spouse, then the decedent essentially is making a transfer of his one-half interest in the community property. The surviving spouse is entitled to the other half of the policy benefit. Regarding inter vivos gifts of community property, one spouse cannot make a lifetime gift of community property without the consent of the other spouse.

rules.rule_statement: Under California law, the surviving spouse or registered domestic partner of an intestate decedent is entitled to the decedent’s one-half of community property and one-half of quasi-community property acquired by the decedent. When combined with the surviving spouse’s own one-half, this means that the surviving spouse or domestic partner will receive all of the community and quasi-community property. The surviving spouse also takes any joint tenancy property shared with the decedent, through the right of survivorship.

When combined with the surviving spouse’s own one-half, this means that the surviving spouse or domestic partner will receive all of the community and quasi-community property. The surviving spouse also takes any joint tenancy property shared with the decedent, through the right of survivorship.

rules.rule_statement: Distribution of the separate property of the decedent will be distributed as follows: i) The spouse gets all of the decedent’s separate property if the decedent is not survived by any lineal descendants, parents, or issue of parents; ii) The spouse gets one-half of the decedent’s separate property if the decedent is survived by one lineal descendant or by a parent or issue of a parent; or iii) The spouse gets one-third of the decedent’s separate property if the decedent is survived by more than one lineal descendent. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 100–101. Cal. Prob. Code § 6401. Cal. Prob. Code § 6401(c).

Separate Property
Distribution of the separate property of the decedent will be distributed as follows: i) The spouse gets all of the decedent’s separate property if the decedent is not survived by any lineal descendants, parents, or issue of parents; ii) The spouse gets one-half of the decedent’s separate property if the decedent is survived by one lineal descendant or by a parent or issue of a parent; or iii) The spouse gets one-third of the decedent’s separate property if the decedent is survived by more than one lineal descendent. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 100–101. Cal. Prob. Code § 6401. Cal. Prob. Code § 6401(c).

rules.rule_statement: At the death of one spouse or domestic partner, one-half of the community property is already owned by the surviving spouse or partner, and only the decedent’s half of community property and quasi-community property is subject to disposition by will. If the spouse who did not acquire the quasi-community property predeceases the acquiring spouse, the non-acquiring spouse has no interest to devise to a third party.

rules.rule_statement: Joint tenancy property held by both spouses is not subject to the special community-property presumption at death, and automatically becomes the property of the surviving spouse through the right of survivorship. See D. Joint Tenancy, infra. Cal. Prob. Code §§ 100–101

rules.rule_statement: Joint tenancy remains at death under the special title presumption and cannot be rebutted by tracing to separate property. Rather, the presumption can be rebutted only by clear and convincing evidence that it was the parties’ intention to classify the property as a non-joint tenancy property (e.g., by agreement). EXAM NOTE: Remember, transmutations made on or after January 1, 1985, must be made in writing by an express declaration by the spouse whose interest in the property is adversely affected. Cal. Fam. Code § 852.

rules.canonical_name: Community Property With Right Of Survivorship
rules.rule_statement: As of July 1, 2001, a husband and wife may hold title as community property with right of survivorship. This means that their property will be treated as community property during marriage and at divorce, but upon the death of one of the spouses, the surviving spouse will own the property through the right of survivorship (e.g., the property will be treated like joint tenancy).

rules.rule_statement: When a testator includes an “election clause” in a will, the surviving spouse must choose between his community property ownership rights or his rights under the will. Election clauses are generally disfavored by the courts.
rules.short_rule_statement: When a testator includes an “election clause” in a will, the surviving spouse must choose between his community property ownership rights or his rights under the will. Election...
rules.source_text:
Election Clause In The Will
When a testator includes an “election clause” in a will, the surviving spouse must choose between his community property ownership rights or his rights under the will. Election clauses are generally disfavored by the courts.

rules.canonical_name: Decedent Transfers Spouse’S Interest In The Will
rules.rule_statement: When there is no election clause in the will but the decedent spouse attempts to transfer the surviving spouse’s interest as well as his own, an election is forced. The statutory elective share does not exist in California and other community-property states. Instead, the surviving spouse or domestic partner is entitled to a forced share of one-half of the community property and quasi-community property. Allowing the spouse to assert both rights would be contrary to the will. The spouse or domestic partner must elect to take the community property share in lieu of all other interests she may have under the testator’s will.

The spouse or domestic partner must elect to take the community property share in lieu of all other interests she may have under the testator’s will.

rules.rule_statement: When a decedent’s will is probated, every debt must first be characterized as either community property or separate property by the probate court, using the same methodology as the divorce court would use in determining the character of property. This allows each debt to be properly allocated. However, when probate is not involved, the surviving spouse becomes personally liable for any debts that should be paid from community property, quasi-community property, or separate property that the surviving spouse is entitled to receive. However, the surviving spouse’s liability extends only to the value of the property. Cal. Prob. Code § 11444.
